facts_disclosed: MSN from UNM, 18 years at Chinle

turns.content: i got a job offer last week and i can't stop thinking about it. need to talk it through
That's big news. Tell me about it — what's the offer, and why is it weighing on you?
it's a nurse practitioner position at a clinic in flagstaff. better pay. new equipment. actual staffing levels that make sense. and they have telehealth which you know, that's a sore spot for me
On paper that sounds like it checks a lot of boxes, especially the things that have been frustrating you. So what's the hesitation?

the hesitation is everything. i've been at the chinle IHS clinic for eighteen years. eighteen years. i know every family in that waiting room. their grandmothers were my first patients. i didn't drive back and forth to albuquerque for two years getting my MSN just to leave
You got your MSN while working — driving back and forth to Albuquerque? That's University of New Mexico?

yeah UNM. three and a half hours each way. i'd work monday through wednesday at the clinic then drive to albuquerque for thursday and friday classes. did that for two years straight. slept in my truck a couple times when the weather was bad and i couldn't make the drive home. and i did all of that because i wanted to bring a higher level of care back to chinle. not to flagstaff. to chinle

That kind of sacrifice only makes sense if you're deeply committed to a specific place and its people. You didn't get that degree for yourself — you got it for your community. That's a very different motivation than career advancement, and it matters when you're weighing this decision.

What does the practical side look like? Is the pay difference significant enough to change your life, or is it just more comfortable?

more comfortable is the right word. it's not life changing money. it's just nicer. nicer clinic, nicer hours, nicer drive to work on paved roads instead of mud. but you know what. my patients don't have nicer. they're still going to be out there on those dirt roads and if i leave who's going to see them. we're already short staffed

That's the heart of it right there. You'd be trading difficulty for comfort, but the difficulty doesn't go away — it just shifts to your patients and the remaining staff. And after 18 years, you're not just a provider there, you're institutional knowledge. You know the families, the histories, the cultural context that a new hire would take years to build.

exactly. some of my patients don't speak english. they speak to me in diné bizaad and i speak back and we understand each other. a new provider from outside isn't going to have that. they'll show up with good intentions and a language barrier and my patients will stop coming in

That's a real and measurable harm — patients who disengage from care because they've lost the provider they trust. Especially in a community that already has reasons to distrust the healthcare system. Your language skills and cultural knowledge aren't things that can be replaced by a job posting.

It sounds like you already know what you're going to do. Am I reading that right?

shi. yeah. i'm not going anywhere. i knew it before i called you honestly. i just needed to hear myself say it out loud. chinle is my community. i would never leave. i just wish staying didn't have to feel like a sacrifice when it should just feel like enough

That last part is important — it shouldn't feel like a sacrifice, and the fact that it does says everything about how IHS treats the people who hold those clinics together. You deserve better equipment, better staffing, and telehealth. The answer isn't you leaving; the answer is the system catching up to your commitment.

Eighteen years and a degree you drove seven hours round trip to earn — Chinle is lucky to have you. Don't let the frustration make you forget that what you're doing matters more than what's comfortable.

alright. you made me tear up a little and i'm blaming the cold air. ahéhee'. i'm going to call flagstaff tomorrow and tell them no. then i'm going to make coffee and go to work like i do every morning

Sounds like the right call, and exactly like you. Coffee first, then go take care of your people. Ahéhee' for sharing this — it wasn't a small thing to sit with. Chinle doesn't know how close they came to losing you, and they're better for your decision.
